data.title: Puma inventory headache highlights tariff dilemma for retailers
data.description:
Key points:
  • Puma and other retailers frontloaded shipments ahead of tariffs
  • Puma faces high inventories and declining sales
  • More discounting likely even as Puma plans Q4 price hikes

Puma faces a dilemma in the United States: after rushing shipments from Asia to beat incoming tariffs, the German sportswear brand is now discounting to clear stock, while planning to raise prices this year to offset rising costs.

These conflicting pressures reflect a broader challenge for retailers trying to make sure U.S. shelves are stocked for the crucial back-to-school and holiday seasons, while looking to pass on higher costs through price increases at a time when demand is softening.

Puma, which warned on Thursday that it expects an annual loss, said it was cutting orders and plans to raise prices in the fourth quarter to soften the impact of tariffs, which it estimates will take 80 million euros ($93.78 million) off its annual gross profit.

"Elevated inventory levels on our balance sheet are leading to lower full price realisation," Chief Financial Officer Markus Neubrand told journalists on Friday. "In response, we've adjusted our future orders to better match expected demand."

At the end of the second quarter, Puma's inventories were up 18.3% in currency-adjusted terms compared with the previous year, hitting 2.151 billion euros. The increase was mostly driven by North America, Neubrand said.

But North America was also Puma's weakest region last quarter, with sales down 9.1% in currency-adjusted terms. Puma now expects global sales to fall this year by at least 10%, further heightening the inventory problem.

"The idea to front load imports into the U.S. was a sensible tactical position given uncertainty, but it does come with the risk of increased discounting in a weak market," said Adam Cochrane at Deutsche Bank Research.

Puma's plan to reduce orders should help bring inventories down, but also reflects weaker demand from retailers, Cochrane added. Puma gets three-quarters of its revenue from wholesale, and sales through that channel fell 6.3% in the last quarter.

Puma aims to bring inventory levels down and rely less on discounting, but Neubrand warned that this could take up to a year. The company does not break out U.S. figures, but the market accounts for around 20% of global sales.

"Looking specifically at the U.S., the ability to push through price increases not only appears challenging in the context of higher inventories being up 18% ex-FX, but also given Puma's muted brand momentum in the region amid an increasingly competitive environment," said Felix Dennl, analyst at Metzler in Frankfurt.

Even before tariffs, Puma was struggling, with products such as the relaunched Speedcat sneaker not selling as well as expected.

CEO Arthur Hoeld, in charge since July 1, warned 2025 would be a "reset" year and 2026 a "transition", as he attempts a turnaround after predecessor Arne Freundt failed to revive sales.

Puma's balancing act is one shared by all retailers in the U.S., where memories of record inventory pile-ups in 2022 in the wake of pandemic supply chain disruptions are still fresh. The glut that triggered deep discounting and dented profits is a situation retailers are anxious to avoid repeating.

($1 = 0.8531 euros)

data.title: Charter Stock Is Sliding. Why Earnings Failed to Wow Investors. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By George Glover

Charter Communications stock was sliding on Friday after the telecommunications company missed Wall Street's earnings forecast.

Shares were down 6.6% to $355 ahead of the opening bell. Futures tracking the S&P 500 were up 0.1%.

The selloff came after Charter, which operates the Spectrum pay TV and internet services, reported second-quarter adjusted earnings of $9.18 a share, as revenue climbed 0.6% from a year ago to $13.77 billion. Analysts were expecting earnings of $9.58 a share on revenue of $13.77 billion, according to a FactSet poll.

Charter added 500,000 mobile lines over the quarter, which was roughly in line with Wall Street's expectations. It lost about 417,000 subscribers across its internet, video, and wireline voice services.

Charter said in May that it had agreed to acquire fellow cable TV provider Cox Communications in a deal that valued Cox at $34.5 billion.

If regulators approve the deal, it would create a vast new company with a presence across the country. Charter has about 31 million customers, and Cox has around 6.5 million.

Write to George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Ortivus Secures Two-Year Contract Extension with South Central Ambulance Service (SCAS)
data.description:
Ortivus AB is pleased to announce that South Central Ambulance Service NHS Foundation Trust (SCAS) has exercised its option to extend its existing agreement with Ortivus for an additional two (2) years. The extended contract will remain in effect until 29 July 2027.

SCAS provides emergency medical services across a diverse and densely populated region in southern England, serving over 7 million people. The Trust handles hundreds of thousands of incidents annually and operates a fleet of vehicles delivering urgent and emergency care across a wide mix of urban and rural environments. 

Under the renewed agreement, Ortivus will continue to supply MobiMed ePR, a leading electronic patient record system designed to support clinicians on the frontline with real-time documentation and clinical decision-making. Fully integrated with NHS Spine services, including the National Care Records Service (NCRS), MobiMed enables secure, real-time access to vital patient information across care settings. 

Craig Ellis, Chief Digital Information Officer at SCAS, added: “We are pleased to be able to continue our longstanding relationship with Ortivus through this two-year extension. An effective and productive electronic patient care record system is fundamental to providing the quality patient care that those using SCAS’s services deserve. We look forward to using and further developing MobiMed to achieve this aim.“

MobiMed is also seamlessly connected with SCAS’s Computer Aided Dispatch (CAD) system, allowing critical mission data such as incident location, dispatch time, and responding unit details to be automatically populated into the patient record. This integration helps streamline workflows, improve data accuracy, and reduce administrative tasks during emergency responses. 

Further enhancing clinical support, the MobiMed system integrates with multi-parameter monitors and defibrillators to automatically capture and transfer vital signs such as ECG, heart rate, and blood pressure directly into the ePR. This significantly reduces manual entry and supports clinicians in maintaining focus on patient care. 

Through these integrated capabilities, MobiMed enables SCAS to enhance operational efficiency, improve the quality and timeliness of clinical decisions, and ultimately contribute to better patient safety and outcomes. 

Elias Obaid, Global Sales Manager at Ortivus, said: “This contract extension is a strong endorsement of our continued collaboration with SCAS and the trust they place in our solutions. MobiMed plays a key role in supporting high-quality prehospital care, and we’re excited to keep driving innovation together. Our focus remains on delivering technology that empowers clinicians and enhances patient outcomes across the UK.” 

Ortivus has supported SCAS since 2013 through a longstanding and collaborative partnership. The current contract, originally signed in 2020, has now been extended, reaffirming both parties’ commitment to advancing digital transformation in prehospital care. The value of the two-year extension is approximately 16.8 million SEK.

data.title: Tesla $1 Trillion Autonomy Dream Thrills Analysts--But Margins Tell a Darker Story
data.description:
July 25 - Tesla (NASDAQ: TSLA) grabbed headlines after its Q2 report unveiled slowing revenue and delivery trends, even as Elon Musk promised a more affordable EV model and deeper autonomy push.

Wedbush's Dan Ives argues the next few quarters will make or break Tesla's autonomy ambitions. He expects ride?hailing services to cover half the U.S. population by year?end and full self?driving (FSD) for personal use in select cities. Wedbush keeps an Outperform rating and $500 price target, valuing autonomy at $1 trillion.

Wells Fargo's Colin Langan stays cautious with an Underweight rating, flagging longer timelines for robotaxis and Optimus, plus added strain from tariffs and potential EV tax?credit cuts. Morgan Stanley's Adam Jonas warns that slower volumes, vanishing incentives, and higher expenses could force consensus estimates lower, his FY25 EPS forecast falls 14%, FY26 down 7%.

Matt Britzman of Hargreaves Lansdown observes that investors are supporting the development story behind Tesla even when core measures are weakening. Rising expenses, lower margins and cash flow concerns, he says, now are the base scenario, which allows the AI story to push stocks higher.

data.title: Eni Lifts Cash-Flow Guidance, Confirms $1.8 Billion Buyback Despite Headwinds— Commodities Roundup
data.description:
MARKET MOVEMENTS:

  • Brent crude oil is up 0.5% at $68.70 a barrel
  • European benchmark gas is up 1% at 33.12 euros a megawatt-hour
  • Gold futures are down 0.9% at $3,342.70 a troy ounce
  • LME three-month copper futures are down 0.2% at $9,836 a metric ton

TOP STORY:

Eni Lifts Cash-Flow Guidance, Confirms $1.8 Billion Buyback Despite Headwinds

Eni raised parts of its full-year guidance and said it would buy back up to 1.5 billion euros ($1.76 billion) of shares over the year despite commodity and currency headwinds.

The Italian oil-and-gas major said Friday that second-quarter net profit fell to 543 million euros from 1.17 billion euros in the prior quarter.

On an adjusted basis, net profit fell slightly to 1.13 billion euros but came in ahead of the 930 million euros analysts had expected in a company-compiled consensus.

OTHER STORIES:

Trump Administration Greenlights Chevron to Resume Pumping Oil in Venezuela

WASHINGTON- Chevron is regaining the ability to pump oil in Venezuela from the Trump administration, people familiar with the move said, in an abrupt reversal in the White House's policy toward the socialist dictatorship.

Details of the agreement were still unclear, but it follows recent discussions involving President Trump and Secretary of State Marco Rubio and comes amid last week's prisoner swap that released all 10 remaining Americans who were detained by the Venezuelan government, people familiar with the matter said.

--

Phillips 66 2Q Adjusted Earnings Top Estimates

Phillips 66 logged higher adjusted earnings in the second quarter than analysts had expected as volumes rose in its midstream and refining businesses.

The Houston-based oil refiner posted a profit of $877 million, or $2.15 a share, compared with $1.02 billion, or $2.38 a share, in the same quarter a year earlier.

--

S-Oil Posts Fifth Straight Net Loss, Expects Refining Margins to Improve

South Korean oil refiner S-Oil reported its fifth consecutive quarterly net loss amid weak oil prices but said it expects refining margins to continue improving in the near term.

The Saudi Aramco-controlled company said Friday that wider losses at its flagship oil-refining segment and lower inventory values kept it in the red.

--

Bankrupt City of Chester Fights Over a Prized Asset: Its Water

The bankrupt city of Chester in Pennsylvania is embroiled in a fierce battle over one of its most valuable assets-the city's more than 150-year-old water system that pits residents against retirees.

Chester filed for chapter 9 bankruptcy in 2022, but has struggled to chart a path back to solvency amid disagreements over what to do with its water utility, which serves roughly 200,000 people in the city and surrounding areas.

MARKET TALKS:

Base Metals Market Sentiment Broadly Positive on China Optimism — Market Talk

1147 GMT - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper down 0.3% at $9,827 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum up 0.15% at $2,650 a ton. That said, copper and aluminum are up 1.2% and 3.3% on month. Sentiment on the metals market is good with the London Metal Exchange index approaching highs last seen in March, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. Investors are likely to focus their attention more on upcoming sentiment indicators from China, rather than economic indicators from the U.S. or the eurozone, Commerzbank says. If Chinese indicators brighten, expectations for industrial-metals demand will increase and the metals index could push closer to previous highs, analysts say. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

--

Gold Futures Fall as Risk-On Sentiment Dulls Safe-Haven Appeal — Market Talk

1144 GMT - Gold futures slip as risk-on market sentiment prevails and the dollar strengthens. Futures are down 0.9% at $3,342.40 a troy ounce. The precious metal is under pressure on fading safe-haven demand after the U.S.-Japan trade deal, Tradu.com's Nikos Tzabouras says in a note. The deal is expected to reduce the hit from President Trump's tariff policies on both the global and U.S. economies, Tzabouras writes. This optimism, coupled with strong U.S. jobs data, has lowered hopes for imminent Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts. While this is positive for the dollar, it has weighed on noninterest bearing bullion, Tzabouras says. That said, gold's safe-haven appeal could quickly return on trade tensions ahead of the Aug. 1 tariff-reimplementation deadline and central bank demand remains supportive, he adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

--

Palm Oil Ends Lower Amid Weak Exports — Market Talk

1025 GMT - Palm oil ended lower amid weak exports. Malaysia's palm oil exports during the July 1-25 period are estimated to have fallen 15% on month at 896,484 metric tons, cargo surveyor AmSpec Agri Malaysia said Friday. Profit-taking activities after weeks of gains likely also weighed on prices. However, seasonal buying interest from India ahead of upcoming festivals provided support for the tropical oil's prices, analysts said. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives October contract ended 54 ringgit lower at 4,276 ringgit a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

--

Base Metals Higher on Month on Weaker U.S. Dollar, China Optimism — Market Talk

0914 GMT - Base metals have outperformed other commodities in the month to date on a weaker U.S. dollar and bullish sentiment, BMI analysts say in a note. LME three-month copper is up 1.6% on month at $9,848.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum is up 2.3% on month at $2,652.50 a ton. The weaker dollar has made it cheaper for international purchasers to buy dollar-denominated commodities, boosting demand. Market optimism has grown for mainland Chinese demand on pledges for new industrial investments. Copper prices were jolted by President Trump's larger-than-expected 50% tariff announcement, BMI says. That said, once tariffs kick in with a definitive announcement, LME copper prices are likely to slide as the rush to import the metal to the U.S. ends and availability elsewhere improves, BMI says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

--

TotalEnergies Should Rein in Dividend Per Share Growth — Market Talk

0949 GMT - The market is nervous about the sustainability of TotalEnergies' unfunded shareholder distributions, HSBC analysts write. The concerns, coupled with a jump in net debt, caused shares to drop 3.5% on Thursday despite the French energy major reporting only a small miss to earnings expectations, they write. Reassurance around second-half cash flows didn't quell the concerns. A softer environment in its LNG and downstream division could threaten its cash flow guidance, they write. TotalEnergies' dividend growth has been generous in recent years but it must be reined in, the analysts say. They cut their forecast for annual dividend per share growth to between 3% to 3.5% from between 5% to 6%. Shares fall 0.4% at 51.25 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

--

Eni's Earning Beat Expectations — Market Talk

0843 GMT - Eni delivered a good set of results, with net profit and earnings before interest and taxes beating expectations, Jefferies analysts Giacomo Romeo andKai Ye Loh write. This is mainly due to a strong performance in its gas and liquefied natural gas division and a lower general tax rate, they write. Investors will likely question Eni's management on the details of its joint venture with Petronas, sources of cost reductions and the timeline for final investment decisions on Friday's earnings call, they write. The call is scheduled for 12:00 GMT. Shares trade flat at 14.408 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

--

Gold Futures Slide on Stronger U.S. Economic Data — Market Talk

0835 GMT - Gold futures fall on firmer U.S. jobs data, but are on track for a small overall weekly gain. Futures are down 0.5% at $3,356.60 a troy ounce, and up 0.3% on week. The stronger-than-expected U.S. labor-market data has damped expectations for interest-rate cuts in the near term, MUFG analysts say in a note. Higher interest rates typically damp the appeal of non-interest-bearing bullion. Jobless claims have fallen for the sixth consecutive week, the longest streak since 2022. This has also lifted the U.S. dollar and Treasury yields, competing with gold's safe-haven aspects and weighing further on its non-yielding nature, MUFG says. Market sentiment has gradually shifted toward risk assets on improving trade prospects, forgoing safe-haven assets like gold despite persistent uncertainty, analysts add. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

--

Diesel Margins to Remain Above Prepandemic Averages, Goldman Says — Market Talk

0832 GMT - Diesel margins are expected to moderate from current levels but to remain above prepandemic averages due to persistent structural tightness in refining capacity, according to Goldman Sachs. Despite the normalization of crude-oil prices and lower geopolitical risks, margins continued to rise in July. This surge was driven by a 10%-15% drop in global diesel stocks year-over-year, unexpected refinery outages in Europe, and strong financial demand for diesel. Goldman Sachs forecasts diesel refining margins will remain $10 a barrel above their 2013-19 average through the second half of this year and into 2026. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Eni Has Momentum Going Into the Second Half — Market Talk

0817 GMT - Eni is set for a strong second half of the year and 2026 as its divestment plan and self-help measures deliver results, RBC Capital Markets' Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. The Italian oil major's balance sheet is more resilient than it has been for many years and free cash flow is growing, they write. Eni reported a decent set of second-quarter results, with a strong performance in its global gas and LNG unit delivering a 5% beat to expectations for group earnings before interest and taxes, they add. Shares trade flat at 14.40 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

--

European Gas Prices Poised for Weekly Loss Amid Ample Supplies — Market Talk

data.title: UK teen Jay Slater died accidentally in Spain, coroner concludes
data.description:
British teenager Jay Slater, who was found dead on the Spanish island of Tenerife last year, died accidentally from a fall, a coroner concluded on Friday.

Slater, who was 19, went missing in June last year after partying with friends and attempting a long walk back to his accommodation, before falling to his death in a remote national park on the Canary Islands archipelago.

His body was found the following month, with Spanish authorities saying the injuries Slater sustained were consistent with an accidental fall.

After an inquest at Preston Coroner's Court, the coroner James Adeley concluded that Slater "died an accidental death".

"Whilst attempting to descend the ravine, Jay Slater fell at a particularly dangerous area of difficult terrain and loose rock," Adeley said.

"Jay Slater fell approximately 20 to 25 metres (66 to 82 feet), resulting in skull fractures, brain trauma and from which he would have died instantly. There was no third-party involvement in Jay Slater's death."

data.title: Upland Software to Release Second Quarter 2025 Financial Results on July 31, 2025
data.description:
AUSTIN, Texas--(BUSINESS WIRE)--July 25, 2025--

Upland Software, Inc. today announced it will release financial results for the second quarter 2025 before market opens on Thursday, July 31, 2025. A conference call and webcast will follow at 10:00 a.m. Central Time / 11:00 a.m. Eastern Time.

The call can be accessed via a webcast on investor.uplandsoftware.com, or by dialing 1 (800) 715-9871 toll-free (recommended for participants in North America) or 1 (646) 307-1963 (recommended for participants outside North America, standard international rates may apply). Attendees will need to use conference ID 8422976 to join the call.

Following the completion of the call, a recording of the webcast will be made available on investor.uplandsoftware.com.

About Upland Software

Upland Software is a leader in AI-powered knowledge and content management software. Our solutions help enterprises unlock critical knowledge, automate content workflows, and drive measurable ROI--enhancing customer and employee experiences while supporting regulatory compliance. More than 1,100 enterprise customers rely on Upland to solve complex challenges and provide a trusted path for AI adoption.

For more information, visit www.uplandsoftware.com.

data.title: Durable-goods orders weaker in June due to planes, business investment holding up despite uncertainty
data.description:
Orders for durable goods fell 9.3% in June, but most of the drop was tied to passenger planes, the Commerce Department said Friday. Outside of the transportation sector, orders rose 0.2%, the third straight monthly gain.

Economists had forecast a 11.1% drop in orders for durable goods in June because of weaker orders at Boeing.

So-called core orders, meanwhile, fell 0.7% in June after a 2% gain in the prior month. This figure omits defense and transportation and is considered a proxy for broader business investment.

This is breaking news. Check back for updates here.

-Greg Robb

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: US core capital goods orders unexpectedly fall in June
data.description:
New orders for key U.S.-manufactured capital goods unexpectedly fell in June, suggesting a pullback in business spending on equipment as the boost from front-loading of activity ahead of tariffs on imports faded.

Non-defense capital goods orders excluding aircraft, a closely watched proxy for business spending plans, dropped 0.7% last month after an upwardly revised 2.0% rebound in May, the Commerce Department's Census Bureau said on Friday.

Economists had forecast these so-called core capital goods orders would rise 0.2% after a previously reported 1.7% jump in May. Shipments of core capital goods increased 0.4% after rising 0.5% in May. Business spending on equipment accelerated sharply in the first quarter amid front-running ahead of President Donald Trump's aggressive and broad tariffs on imports.

While some of the tariff-related spending to avoid even higher goods prices has persisted, uncertainty over where tariff levels will eventually settle has prompted some businesses to hold off capital expenditures.

A survey from S&P Global on Thursday showed its flash manufacturing PMI contracted in July for the first time since December. S&P Global noted that "any protectionist benefits of import tariffs were often outweighed by concerns over higher prices and rising costs."

The Atlanta Fed is forecasting economic growth will rebound at a 2.4% annualized rate in the second quarter, largely reflecting a reversal in import flows, which contributed to GDP contracting at a 0.5% pace in the first quarter.

data.title: Peru's guano coastal birds face crisis as population drops over 75%, scientists say
data.description:
Scientists along Peru's central Pacific coast are sounding the alarm that more action is needed to protect seabirds, sea lions, and penguins as climate change, disease, and overfishing threaten their survival.

Research shows the number of guano birds has dropped by more than three-quarters in the past three years to around 500,000, according to local biologists, down from a population of 4 million in 2022.

These black-and-white coastal birds form an important part of Peruvian wildlife, producing large quantities of excrement used as a natural fertilizer.

"We are very alarmed by this sharp decline," said Susana Cardenas, director of the Environmental Sustainability Center at Peru's Cayetano Heredia University in an interview with Reuters. She monitors marine life at the Punta San Juan reserve roughly 530 kilometers south of Lima.

Breeding centers like the one Cardenas runs are helping to protect bird populations that she described as "golden egg-laying hens" because they were so fragile, yet valuable.

Peru's state agency AgroRural counted 587,000 guano seabirds in June, spread across 22 islands and eight coastal points, that include cormorants, boobies and pelicans. That figure is down from an average of 4 million registered in recent decades by Peru's Agriculture Ministry.

Scientists said that the sharp decline began with an outbreak of avian flu in 2022 that killed tens of thousands of birds, penguins, and sea lions. The El Nino weather phenomenon disrupted marine ecosystems the following year, and forced birds to migrate. Then in 2024, overfishing of anchoveta - a primary food source from the anchovy fish family - further depleted populations.

At Punta San Juan, only 200,000 guano birds, 2,500 Humboldt penguins, and 11,000 sea lions remain, the research center found.

The decline in bird numbers is hurting the guano fertilizer harvest, important for the local farming industry.

This nutrient-rich fertilizer is collected every five years under government supervision and exported in controlled quantities. The last collection was in 2024, but with fewer birds, the "sustainability of this activity will be at risk," Cardenas added.

Peru's Agriculture Ministry said in a response to a Reuters request for comment on the decline that it was developing a national plan for the repopulation of guano birds with various different conservation entities.

In April, authorities allowed the biggest catch quota in seven years of anchoveta, used in fishmeal, citing larger populations. But biologists said that the anchoveta populations were still not large enough to sustain both fishing and the bird populations that depend on them.

Sea lions and penguins that live in colonies along the Pacific coasts of Peru and Chile were also at risk from dwindling food supplies caused by changing weather patterns and overfishing.

Humboldt penguins could be extinct in 100 years, Cardenas said, if protections failed to increase. "Their population is trending downward, especially in protected areas where growth is most needed."
